was an agreement between the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") on 2 September 1940, according to which 50 [*Caldwell*](/wiki/Caldwell-class_destroyer "Caldwell-class destroyer"), [*Wickes*](/wiki/Wickes-class_destroyer "Wickes-class destroyer"), and [*Clemson*](/wiki/Clemson-class_destroyer "Clemson-class destroyer")-class US Navy destroyers were transferred to the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") from the [US Navy](/wiki/US_Navy "US Navy") in exchange for land rights on [British possessions](/wiki/British_possessions "British possessions"). At the time, the United States was neutral in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

Destroyers-for-bases deal

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Transferred destroyers sailing into British port, October 1940 | |
| Signed | 2 September 1940 |
| Signatories | * [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") [Franklin Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_Roosevelt "Franklin Roosevelt") * [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill") |
| Parties | * [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") * [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") |
| Languages | English |

The **destroyers-for-bases deal** was an agreement between the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") on 2 September 1940, according to which 50 [*Caldwell*](/wiki/Caldwell-class_destroyer "Caldwell-class destroyer"), [*Wickes*](/wiki/Wickes-class_destroyer "Wickes-class destroyer"), and [*Clemson*](/wiki/Clemson-class_destroyer "Clemson-class destroyer")-class US Navy destroyers were transferred to the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") from the [US Navy](/wiki/US_Navy "US Navy") in exchange for land rights on [British possessions](/wiki/British_possessions "British possessions"). At the time, the United States was neutral in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

Generally referred to as the "twelve hundred-ton type" (also known as "flush-deck", or "four-pipers" after their four [funnels](/wiki/Funnel_(ship) "Funnel (ship)")), the destroyers became the British [Town class](/wiki/Town-class_destroyer "Town-class destroyer") and were named after towns common to both countries.[[1]](#cite_note-1) US President [Franklin Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_Roosevelt "Franklin Roosevelt") used an [executive agreement](/wiki/Executive_agreement "Executive agreement"), which does not require congressional approval. He was sharply criticised from antiwar Americans, who took the position that the agreement violated the [Neutrality Acts](/wiki/Neutrality_Acts_of_the_1930s "Neutrality Acts of the 1930s").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Destroyers-for-bases_deal&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

By late June 1940, France had surrendered to [Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and Italy. The [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") and the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") stood alone in warfare against Hitler and Mussolini. The British [Chiefs of Staff Committee](/wiki/Chiefs_of_Staff_Committee "Chiefs of Staff Committee") concluded in May that if [France collapsed](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France"), "we do not think we could continue the war with any chance of success" without "full economic and financial support" from the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[3]](#cite_note-reynolds1993-3) The US government was sympathetic to Britain's plight, but US public opinion overwhelmingly supported [isolationism](/wiki/United_States_non-interventionism "United States non-interventionism") to avoid involvement in "another European war". Reflecting that sentiment, the [US Congress](/wiki/US_Congress "US Congress") had passed the [Neutrality Acts](/wiki/Neutrality_Acts_of_1930s "Neutrality Acts of 1930s") three years earlier, which banned the shipment or sale of arms from the US to any combatant nation. US President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") was further constrained because [1940 Presidential election](/wiki/1940_United_States_presidential_election "1940 United States presidential election") was due, as his critics sought to portray him as being pro-war. Legal advice from the [US Justice Department](/wiki/US_Justice_Department "US Justice Department") stated that the transaction was legal.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

By late May, the evacuation of British forces from [Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk "Dunkirk"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), in [Operation Dynamo](/wiki/Operation_Dynamo "Operation Dynamo") caused the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") to need ships immediately, especially as it was fighting the [Battle of the Atlantic](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Atlantic "Battle of the Atlantic") in which German [U-boats](/wiki/U-boat "U-boat") threatened the British supplies of food and of other resources essential to the war effort. With German troops advancing rapidly into France and many in the US government convinced that the defeat of France and Britain was imminent, the US sent a proposal to London through the British ambassador, the [Marquess of Lothian](/wiki/Philip_Kerr,_11th_Marquess_of_Lothian "Philip Kerr, 11th Marquess of Lothian"), for an American lease of airfields on [Trinidad](/wiki/Trinidad "Trinidad"), [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda") and [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_(island) "Newfoundland (island)").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The Prime Minister [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill") initially rejected the offer on 27 May unless Britain received something immediate in return. On 1 June, as the defeat of France loomed, Roosevelt bypassed the Neutrality Act by declaring as "surplus" many millions of rounds of US ammunition and obsolescent small arms and authorising their shipment to Britain. Roosevelt rejected Churchill's pleas for destroyers for the Royal Navy. By August, while Britain was reaching a low point, US Ambassador [Joseph P. Kennedy](/wiki/Joseph_P._Kennedy "Joseph P. Kennedy") reported from London that a British surrender was "inevitable". Seeking to persuade Roosevelt to send the destroyers, Churchill warned Roosevelt that if Britain were vanquished, its colonial islands close to American shores could become a direct threat to the US if they fell into German hands.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Deal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Destroyers-for-bases_deal&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Deal")]

Roosevelt approved the deal on the evening of 30 August 1940.[[6]](#cite_note-goodhart1965-6) On 2 September 1940, as the [Battle of Britain](/wiki/Battle_of_Britain "Battle of Britain") intensified, Secretary of State [Cordell Hull](/wiki/Cordell_Hull "Cordell Hull") signaled agreement to the transfer of the warships to the Royal Navy. On 3 September 1940, Admiral [Harold Stark](/wiki/Harold_Rainsford_Stark "Harold Rainsford Stark") certified that the destroyers were not vital to US security. In exchange, the US was granted land in various [British possessions](/wiki/British_possessions "British possessions") for the establishment of naval or air bases with rent-free [99-year leases](/wiki/99-year_lease "99-year lease"), on:[[6]](#cite_note-goodhart1965-6)

* [Newfoundland](/wiki/Dominion_of_Newfoundland "Dominion of Newfoundland")
* Eastern side of the [Bahamas](/wiki/Bahamas "Bahamas")
* Southern coast of [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica")
* Western coast of [Saint Lucia](/wiki/Saint_Lucia "Saint Lucia")
* West coast of Trinidad ([Gulf of Paria](/wiki/Gulf_of_Paria "Gulf of Paria"))
* [Antigua](/wiki/Antigua "Antigua")
* [British Guiana](/wiki/British_Guiana "British Guiana") (now [Guyana](/wiki/Guyana "Guyana")) within fifty miles of [Georgetown](/wiki/Georgetown,_Guyana "Georgetown, Guyana")

The agreement also granted the US air and naval base rights in:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* The [Great Sound](/wiki/Great_Sound,_Bermuda "Great Sound, Bermuda") and [Castle Harbour](/wiki/Castle_Harbour,_Bermuda "Castle Harbour, Bermuda"), [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda")
* South and eastern coasts of Newfoundland

No destroyers were received in exchange for the bases in Bermuda and Newfoundland. Both territories were vital to trans-Atlantic shipping, aviation, and the [Battle of the Atlantic](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Atlantic "Battle of the Atlantic"). Although an attack on either territory was unlikely, it could not be discounted and Britain had been forced wastefully to maintain defensive forces, including the [Bermuda Garrison](/wiki/Bermuda_Garrison "Bermuda Garrison"). The deal allowed Britain to hand much of the defence of Bermuda to the neutral US, which freed British forces for redeployment to more active theatres and enabled the development of strategic facilities at US expense, which British forces would also use.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Wickes_class_destroyers_before_transfer_to_the_UK_1940.JPG/250px-Wickes_class_destroyers_before_transfer_to_the_UK_1940.JPG)](/wiki/File:Wickes_class_destroyers_before_transfer_to_the_UK_1940.JPG)

American and British sailors examine depth charges. In the background are US [*Wickes*-class destroyers](/wiki/Wickes-class_destroyer "Wickes-class destroyer") before their transfer

The [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") (RAF) and the [Fleet Air Arm](/wiki/Fleet_Air_Arm "Fleet Air Arm") (FAA) maintained air stations in Bermuda at the start of the war, but they served only flying boats. The [RAF station](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force,_Bermuda,_1939-1945 "Royal Air Force, Bermuda, 1939-1945") on [Darrell's Island](/wiki/Darrell%27s_Island,_Bermuda "Darrell's Island, Bermuda") served as a staging point for trans-Atlantic flights by [RAF Transport Command](/wiki/RAF_Transport_Command "RAF Transport Command") and [RAF Ferry Command](/wiki/RAF_Ferry_Command "RAF Ferry Command"), [BOAC](/wiki/British_Overseas_Airways_Corporation "British Overseas Airways Corporation"), and [Pan-Am](/wiki/Pan-American_World_Airways "Pan-American World Airways") and hosted the [Bermuda Flying School](/wiki/Bermuda_Flying_School "Bermuda Flying School"), but it did not operate maritime patrols. The [FAA station](/wiki/RNAS_Boaz_Island_(HMS_Malabar) "RNAS Boaz Island (HMS Malabar)") on [Boaz Island](/wiki/Boaz_Island,_Bermuda "Boaz Island, Bermuda") serviced aircraft based on vessels operating from or through the [Royal Naval Dockyard](/wiki/Royal_Naval_Dockyard,_Bermuda "Royal Naval Dockyard, Bermuda"), but it attempted to maintain maritime patrols by using pilots from naval ships, RAF Darrell's Island and the Bermuda Flying School.

The agreement for bases in Bermuda stipulated that the US would, at its own expense, build an airfield capable of handling large landplanes that would be operated jointly by the US Army Air Force and the Royal Air Force. The airfield was named [Kindley Field](/wiki/Kindley_Field "Kindley Field") after [Field Kindley](/wiki/Field_Kindley "Field Kindley"), an American aviator who fought for Britain during World War I. RAF Transport Command relocated its operations to the airfield when it was completed in 1943, but RAF Ferry Command remained at Darrell's Island. The US Navy had established the [Naval Operating Base](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Bermuda_Annex "Naval Air Station Bermuda Annex") at Bermuda's West End, a flying boat station from which maritime patrols were operated for the remainder of the war (the US Navy had actually begun operating such patrols from RAF Darrell's Island by using floatplanes and was waiting for their own base to become operational). The RAF and FAA facilities were closed after the war, which left only the US air bases in Bermuda. The Naval Operating Base ceased to be an air station in 1965, when its flying boats were replaced by [Lockheed P-2 Neptunes](/wiki/Lockheed_P-2_Neptune "Lockheed P-2 Neptune") operating from the [Kindley Air Force Base](/wiki/Kindley_Air_Force_Base "Kindley Air Force Base") (as the former US Army airfield had become). Those US air bases were in fact only two of several [US military facilities](/wiki/Military_of_Bermuda#Former_US_Bases_In_Bermuda "Military of Bermuda") that operated in Bermuda during the 20th century. In spite of the 99-year lease, the US abandoned many of the bases in 1949, and the remaining few were closed by 1995.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The US accepted the "generous action... to enhance the national security of the United States" and immediately transferred in return 50 *Caldwell*, *Wickes*, and *Clemson*-class U.S. Navy destroyers, "generally referred to as the twelve hundred-ton type" (also known as "flush-deckers", or "four-pipers" after their four [funnels](/wiki/Funnel_(ship) "Funnel (ship)")). Forty-three ships initially went to the Royal Navy and seven to the [Royal Canadian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Navy "Royal Canadian Navy"). In the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") navies, the ships were renamed after towns and so were known as the "Town" class, but they had originally belonged to three classes (*Caldwell*, *Wickes*, and *Clemson*). Before the end of the war, nine others had also served with the Royal Canadian Navy. Five Towns were manned by [Royal Norwegian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Norwegian_Navy "Royal Norwegian Navy") crews, with the survivors later returned to the Royal Navy. [HMS *Campbeltown*](/wiki/HMS_Campbeltown_(I42) "HMS Campbeltown (I42)") was manned by [Royal Netherlands Navy](/wiki/Royal_Netherlands_Navy "Royal Netherlands Navy") sailors before her assignment to the [St. Nazaire Raid](/wiki/St._Nazaire_Raid "St. Nazaire Raid"). Nine other destroyers were eventually transferred to the [Soviet Navy](/wiki/Soviet_Navy "Soviet Navy"). Six of the 50 destroyers were lost to [U-boats](/wiki/U-boat "U-boat"), and three others, including *Campbeltown*, were destroyed in other circumstances.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Britain had no choice but to accept the deal, but it was so much more advantageous to the United States than Britain that Churchill's aide [John Colville](/wiki/John_Colville_(civil_servant) "John Colville (civil servant)") compared it to the [USSR's relationship with Finland](/wiki/Interim_Peace "Interim Peace"). The destroyers were [in reserve](/wiki/United_States_Navy_reserve_fleets "United States Navy reserve fleets") from the massive US shipbuilding program during World War I, and many of the vessels required extensive overhaul because they had not been preserved properly while inactivated. British [Admiral](/wiki/Admiral_(Royal_Navy) "Admiral (Royal Navy)") [Bertram Ramsay](/wiki/Bertram_Ramsay "Bertram Ramsay") called them the "worst destroyers I had ever seen",[[7]](#cite_note-olson2010-7)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] and only 30 were in service by May 1941.[[3]](#cite_note-reynolds1993-3) Churchill also disliked the deal, but his advisers persuaded him merely to tell Roosevelt,

> We have so far only been able to bring a few of your fifty destroyers into action on account of the many defects which they naturally develop when exposed to Atlantic weather after having been laid up so long.[[7]](#cite_note-olson2010-7)[[7]](#cite_note-olson2010-7)

Roosevelt responded by transferring ten [Lake-class](/wiki/Banff-class_sloop "Banff-class sloop") [Coast Guard cutters](/wiki/Coast_Guard_cutter "Coast Guard cutter") to the Royal Navy in 1941. The [United States Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard") vessels were ten years younger than the destroyers and had greater range, which made them more useful as anti-submarine convoy escorts.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The agreement was much more important for being the start of the wartime Anglo-American partnership. Churchill said in the [British Parliament](/wiki/British_Parliament "British Parliament") that "these two great organisations of the English-speaking democracies, the British Empire and the United States, will have to be somewhat mixed up together in some of their affairs for mutual and general advantage".[[3]](#cite_note-reynolds1993-3)
